analogy: Think of "Units and Measurements" through the chapter's own opening images: CHAPTER TWO MOTION IN A STRAIGHT LINE 2.1 Introduction 2.2 Instantaneous velocity and speed 2.3 Acceleration 2.4 Kinematic equations for uniformly accelerated motion 2.5 Relative velocity Summary Points to ponder Exercises 2.1 INTRODUCTION Motion is common to everything in the universe. Even when we are... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Units and Measurements is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that cHAPTER TWO MOTION IN A STRAIGHT LINE 2.1 Introduction 2.2 Instantaneous velocity and speed 2.3 Acceleration 2.4 Kinematic equations for uniformly accelerated motion 2.5 Relative velocity Summary Points to ponder Exercises 2.1 INTRODUCTION Motion is common to everything in the universe.

The text explains that even when we are sleeping, air moves into and out of our lungs and blood flows in arteries and veins.

The text explains that we see leaves falling from trees and water flowing down a dam.

The text explains that automobiles and planes carry people from one place to the other.

The text explains that the earth rotates once every twenty-four hours and revolves round the sun once in a year.

The text explains that the Sun itself is in motion in the Milky Way, which is again moving within its local group of galaxies.

The text explains that motion is change in position of an object with time.

The text explains that in this chapter, we shall learn how to describe motion.

The text explains that for this, we develop the concepts of velocity and acceleration.

The text explains that we shall confine ourselves to the study of motion of objects along a straight line, also known as rectilinear motion.

The text explains that for the case of rectilinear motion with uniform acceleration, a set of simple equations can be obtained.

The text explains that finally, to understand the relative nature of motion, we introduce the concept of relative velocity.

The text explains that in our discussions, we shall treat the objects in motion as point objects.

The text explains that this approximation is valid so far as the size of the object is much smaller than the distance it moves in a reasonable duration of time.

The text explains that in a good number of situations in real-life, the size of objects can be neglected and they can be considered as point-like objects without much error.

The text explains that in Kinematics, we study ways to describe motion without going into the causes of motion.

The text explains that (a) Motion in positive direction with positive acceleration, (b) Motion in positive direction with negative acceleration, (c) Motion in negative direction with negative acceleration, (d) Motion of an object with negative acceleration that changes direction at time t1.

The text explains that between times 0 to t1, it moves in positive x - direction and between t1 and t2 it moves in the opposite direction.

The text explains that acceleration, therefore, may result from a change in speed (magnitude), a change in direction or changes in both.

The text explains that 2.3 shows velocity- time graph for motion with constant acceleration for the following cases : (a) An object is moving in a positive direction with a positive acceleration.

The text explains that (b) An object is moving in positive direction with a negative acceleration.

The text explains that (c) An object is moving in negative direction with a negative acceleration.

The text explains that (d) An object is moving in positive direction till time t1, and then turns back with the same negative acceleration.

The text explains that answer (a) Let us take the y-axis in the vertically upward direction with zero at the ground, as shown in Fig.

The text explains that we assume that the motion is in y-direction, more correctly in –y-direction because we choose upward direction as positive.

The text explains that since the acceleration due to gravity is always downward, it is in the negative direction and we have a = – g = – 9.8 m s–2 The object is released from rest at y = 0.

The text explains that the origin and the positive direction of an axis are a matter of choice.

The text explains that if a particle is speeding up, acceleration is in the direction of velocity; if its speed is decreasing, acceleration is in the direction opposite to that of the velocity.

The text explains that the sign of acceleration (as mentioned in point 3) depends on the choice of the positive direction of the axis.

The text explains that for example, if the vertically upward direction is chosen to be the positive direction of the axis, the acceleration due to gravity is negative.

The text explains that (2.9)) are true only for motion in which the magnitude and the direction of acceleration are constant during the course of motion.

The text explains that (a) What is the direction of acceleration during the upward motion of the ball ?

The text explains that (c) Choose the x = 0 m and t = 0 s to be the location and time of the ball at its highest point, vertically downward direction to be the positive direction of x-axis, and give the signs of position, velocity and acceleration of the ball during its upward, and downward motion.

The text explains that 2.14 A police van moving on a highway with a speed of 30 km h–1 fires a bullet at a thief’s car speeding away in the same direction with a speed of 192 km h–1.

The text explains that 2.18 Figure 2.15 gives a speed-time graph of a particle in motion along a constant direction.

The text explains that choosing the positive direction as the constant direction of motion, give the signs of v and a in the three intervals.

The text explains that give the signs of position, velocity and acceleration variables of the particle at t = 0.3 s, 1.2 s, – 1.2 s.

The text explains that 2.13 2.17 Figure 2.14 gives the x-t plot of a particle in one-dimensional motion.

The text explains that three different equal intervals of time are shown.

The text explains that in which interval is the average speed greatest, and in which is it the least ?

The text explains that give the sign of average velocity for each interval.

The text explains that in which interval is the average acceleration greatest in magnitude?

The text explains that in which interval is the average speed greatest ?

The text explains that what are the accelerations at the points A, B, C and D ?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Physics, Chapter 2: Units and Measurements

prelims_traps: Do not treat CHAPTER TWO and MOTION IN A STRAIGHT LINE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Units and Measurements; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Units and Measurements through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CHAPTER TWO and MOTION IN A STRAIGHT LINE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
